quiz.title: Unit 9 Quiz 12, Grade 10 Biology
quiz.subtitle: Genetics: Test Crosses, Gamete Formation, and Advanced Crosses
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Test Cross, Incomplete Dominance, Sex-Linked Inheritance, Dihybrid Crosses, Gamete Types, Linked Genes, Probability Calculations

questions.question: If a scientist surgically removes the tails of a population of laboratory mice and then allows them to interbreed for 20 generations, which of the following statements regarding the 21st generation would be true?
options: All mice in the 21st generation will be born without tails.
Some mice in the 21st generation will be born without tails due to inheritance of the acquired trait.
Mice in the 21st generation will be born with normal tails, unless a genetic mutation occurs.
The average tail length of the mice will gradually decrease over generations.
questions.explanation: Acquired characteristics (surgically removed tails) are not inherited. Lamarck's theory of inheritance of acquired characteristics has been disproven. Mice will continue to be born with normal tails unless random genetic mutations affect tail development.

questions.question: A plant with red flowers is crossed with a plant having white flowers. The flower color is controlled by a single gene, but in this species, the trait shows incomplete dominance. The F₁ generation results in offspring that all have pink flowers. These F₁ plants are then self-pollinated to produce the F₂ generation. Given this information, what is the expected phenotypic ratio of the F₂ plants?
options: Red : white in the ratio of 3 : 1
Red : pink : white in the ratio of 1 : 2 : 1
Red : pink : white in the ratio of 3 : 1 : 1
questions.explanation: In incomplete dominance, F₁ (pink) are heterozygous (Rr). F₁ self-cross (Rr × Rr) gives genotypic ratio 1 RR : 2 Rr : 1 rr, corresponding to phenotypic ratio 1 red : 2 pink : 1 white.

questions.question: The gene for the genetic disease 'Haemophilia' is present on the 'X' chromosome. If a haemophilic male marries a normal female, what would be the probability of their son being haemophilic?
questions.explanation: Haemophilic male = XʰY, normal female = XᴴXᴴ. All daughters get Xʰ from father and Xᴴ from mother → XᴴXʰ (carriers). All sons get Y from father and Xᴴ from mother → XᴴY (normal). Probability of haemophilic son = 0.

questions.question: A Mendelian experiment consisted of breeding tall pea plants bearing violet flowers with short pea plants bearing white flowers. The progeny all bear violet flowers, but almost half of them were short. This suggests that the genetic make-up of the tall parent can be depicted as
questions.explanation: All progeny have violet flowers → violet is dominant and parent must be homozygous dominant for flower color (WW). Half are short → tall parent must be heterozygous for height (Tt). So tall parent = TtWW. Short parent = ttww.

questions.question: In sheep, a dominant allele (B) produces black hair and a recessive allele (b) produces white hair. If you saw a black sheep, you would be able to identify:
options: the genotypes for only one of its parents.
the genotypes for both of its parents.
questions.explanation: Black sheep could be either BB or Bb (both produce black phenotype). You can identify phenotype (black) but not genotype without a test cross. Parental genotypes cannot be determined from just observing one offspring.

questions.question: An organism has the genotype AaBbCc. How many different types of gametes can this organism produce assuming the genes assort independently?
questions.explanation: Number of heterozygous gene pairs = 3 (Aa, Bb, Cc). Number of gamete types = 2ⁿ = 2³ = 8. Each gene contributes 2 possibilities, and they assort independently.

questions.question: Why is a homozygous recessive plant (aabb) used in a test cross rather than a homozygous dominant plant (AABB)?
options: Recessive alleles are stronger than dominant alleles during fertilization.
The recessive alleles allow any dominant alleles from the other parent to be visible in the phenotype.
A dominant parent would make all offspring look different, causing confusion.
Homozygous recessive plants are easier to grow in a lab setting.
questions.explanation: Test cross uses homozygous recessive because recessive alleles do not mask the alleles from the other parent. When crossed with unknown genotype, the offspring phenotypes directly reveal what alleles the unknown parent contributed.

questions.question: In a dihybrid cross (AaBb × aabb), what is the expected phenotypic ratio if the genes are assorting independently?
questions.explanation: AaBb × aabb is a test cross. AaBb produces four gamete types (AB, Ab, aB, ab) in equal proportions (1/4 each). aabb produces only ab gametes. Offspring phenotypes directly reflect gamete types from AaBb, giving 1:1:1:1 ratio.

questions.question: If you cross AaBb with aabb and find that the offspring are 50% AaBb and 50% aabb, what does this suggest about the genes A and B?
options: One gene is dominant over the other.
They are linked on the same chromosome.
questions.explanation: If genes assort independently, AaBb × aabb should produce four phenotypes in equal 1:1:1:1 ratio. Getting only two parental types (AaBb and aabb) indicates linkage—the genes are on the same chromosome and do not assort independently.

questions.question: A researcher performs a test cross with a purple-flowered pea plant of unknown genotype (P_). All 50 offspring produced have purple flowers. What is the most likely genotype of the parent?
questions.explanation: Test cross with homozygous recessive (pp). If unknown parent were Pp, expect 50% purple (Pp) and 50% white (pp). All 50 offspring purple suggests unknown parent is PP (homozygous dominant), producing all Pp offspring.

questions.question: The tendency of an offspring to resemble its parent is known as
questions.explanation: Heredity is the passing of traits from parents to offspring, resulting in resemblance between generations. Phenotype refers to observable traits, genetics is the study of heredity, and inheritance is the process of receiving traits, but heredity specifically refers to the tendency of offspring to resemble parents.

questions.question: Which of the following represents a gamete produced by a plant with genotype AaBb?
questions.explanation: Gametes are haploid and contain one allele from each gene. Ab contains one allele from A (A) and one from B (b). Aa, AABB, and Bb are diploid combinations not found in gametes.

questions.question: If a plant with genotype AaBb is self-pollinated, how many squares would a Punnett square require to show all possible zygote combinations?
questions.explanation: AaBb produces 4 gamete types (AB, Ab, aB, ab). A 4×4 Punnett square has 16 boxes, representing all possible zygote combinations from self-pollination.

questions.question: In a cross between AABb and aaBb, what proportion of the offspring are expected to be homozygous recessive for both traits (aabb)?
questions.explanation: A locus: AA × aa → all Aa (no aa possible). Therefore, probability of aabb = 0 regardless of B locus. For aabb, need aa from A locus, which is impossible here.

questions.question: In order to find out different types of gametes produced by a pea plant having the genotype AaBb, it should be crossed to a plant with the genotype:
questions.explanation: To determine gamete types from AaBb, perform a test cross with homozygous recessive (aabb). The offspring phenotypes directly reveal what gametes the AaBb parent produced. This is Mendel's test cross method.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered test crosses and advanced genetics! 🧬
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review gamete formation and dihybrid test cross ratios.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on linked genes and test cross applications.
scoreMessages.0: Please review test cross logic, gamete calculation, and incomplete dominance.
